case_name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd v H.A. & Chung Partnership and others

audit_suggestion.case_name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd v H.A. & Chung Partnership and others

label_change.evidence: The plaintiff APG succeeded on its claims; the court granted judgment to the plaintiff against the defendants in paragraph 93.
label_change.recommended_change: Replace the non-standard label 'Allowed' with the required plaintiff outcome label 'Claim Allowed'.

fact_changes.current_value: On incorporation, APG issued fully paid-up ordinary shares as follows: 360,000 shares to Sean Colville Niven, 160,000 shares to Wang Zijian (James Wang), 40,000 shares to Gan Siong Teck, 200,000 shares to Hou Junyu and 40,000 shares to Lee Jer Ren, although the consideration for these shares had not in fact been paid.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On incorporation, APG had the following directors and shareholders: Sean Colville Niven (360,000 ordinary shares), Wang Zijian (160,000 ordinary shares), Gan Siong Teck (40,000 ordinary shares), Hou Junyu (200,000 ordinary shares) and Lee Jer Ren (40,000 ordinary shares). The shares were issued as fully paid-up, but in fact had not been paid for.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 6 states the directors/shareholders and shareholdings, and separately notes that although the shares were issued as fully paid-up, in fact they had not been paid for.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Separate the shareholding list from the unpaid consideration point and avoid implying the shares themselves were 'issued' as fully paid-up in the same sentence.

fact_changes.current_value: In December 2008, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian decided to start a new company to sell Australian properties and resigned from JL Property Group Pte Ltd.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In December 2008, the defendants decided to start their own company to sell Australian properties. They resigned from JL Property Group Pte Ltd and approached Goh Cheng Liang for a loan of $900,000.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 5 says the defendants decided to start their own company, resigned from JL Property, and approached Goh Cheng Liang for a loan of $900,000.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Restore the loan-approach fact and use the source's wording that the defendants decided to start their own company.

fact_changes.current_value: APG received $900,000 from Goh Cheng Liang as start-up capital funding pursuant to the 11 March 2009 loan agreement.
fact_changes.recommended_value: APG received the loan of $900,000 pursuant to a loan agreement dated 11 March 2009 with Goh Cheng Liang.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 7 states that APG received the loan of $900,000 pursuant to a loan agreement dated 11 March 2009 with Mr Goh.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Avoid characterising the loan as 'start-up capital funding'; the source describes it as a loan.

fact_changes.current_value: Goh Cheng Liang was the godfather of Wang Zijian and provided loan funding to APG.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Goh Cheng Liang was the god-father of Wang Zijian.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 5 states that Mr Goh was the god-father of Mr Wang.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported restatement that he 'provided loan funding to APG' from this relationship fact, since that is already covered elsewhere.

fact_changes.current_value: Clause 5 of the 11 March 2009 loan agreement with Goh Cheng Liang stated that until the loan was fully repaid, APG would not make payment of any director's fees, remuneration or benefits to any director, except salaries payable in the ordinary course to the executive directors.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Clause 5 of the loan agreement provided that until the loan had been fully repaid, APG was not to make payment of any director's fees, remuneration or any benefits to any director, save and except for salaries payable in the ordinary course to the defendants in their capacity as executive directors.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 62 quotes clause 5 in substance and refers to salaries payable in the ordinary course to the defendants in their capacity as executive directors.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording more closely and avoid narrowing the exception to 'any director' when the text refers to the defendants as executive directors.

fact_changes.current_value: In November 2009, APG borrowed funds from Ubertas Funds Management Pty Ltd, an Australian company, and used those funds to repay the $900,000 loan from Goh Cheng Liang.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In November 2009, APG borrowed money from an Australian company, Ubertas Funds Management Pty Ltd, and used it to repay Mr Goh's loan.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 10 states that APG borrowed money from Ubertas and used it to repay Mr Goh's loan.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported exact phrasing 'borrowed funds' and keep the source's wording.

fact_changes.current_value: In January 2010, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene purchased 240,000 APG shares from Sean Colville Niven for $160,000.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In January 2010, she became a shareholder of the company when she purchased 240,000 shares from Mr Niven for $160,000.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 10 states that in January 2010 she became a shareholder when she purchased 240,000 shares from Mr Niven for $160,000.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'APG shares' if not necessary.

fact_changes.current_value: On 2 July 2010, John Kennedy Stuart sent an email to APG's directors stating that, based on APG's cash flow difficulties, existing and pending financial commitments and expected receipts, APG was technically insolvent as at that date.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 2 July 2010, Mr Stuart advised the directors of APG that, in his view, APG was technically insolvent.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 11 says Mr Stuart advised the directors on 2 July 2010 that APG was technically insolvent.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the detailed paraphrase of the email contents and keep only what is explicitly stated in the source.

fact_changes.current_value: On 2 July 2010, after receiving John Kennedy Stuart's email, Sean Colville Niven replied by email stating that the creditors report and revenue report had been visible to the shareholders for at least six months and that there was nothing new in the summary of APG's cash flow and creditors' position.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 2 July 2010, Mr Niven sent a response in which he seemed to accept the analysis as not only being correct but something which all the directors should have been aware of.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 74 states that Mr Niven's email said there was nothing in Mr Stuart's email that should come as a shock and that the creditors report and revenue report had been visible for at least six months.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's more limited description and avoid over-quoting or adding 'shareholders' where the source refers to directors.

fact_changes.current_value: In July 2010, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene injected $25,000 into APG to help fund a marketing event.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In July 2010, Ms Lim had to put in a cash injection of $25,000 to help the company fund a marketing event.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 76 states that Ms Lim had to put in a cash injection of $25,000 in July 2010 to help the company fund a marketing event.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid naming her as 'Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene' if the source uses 'Ms Lim' in this context.

fact_changes.current_value: On 18 July 2010, Sean Colville Niven sent an email stating that APG had faced financial pressure every month since repaying the loan to Goh Cheng Liang and committing to monthly repayments of A$40,000 to Ubertas.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 18 July 2010, Mr Niven placed the blame for the difficult cash flow position which APG faced on its repayment of the loan to Mr Goh and its subsequent commitment to repay A$40,000 to Ubertas every month.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 75 states that Mr Niven placed the blame for APG's difficult cash flow position on repayment of Mr Goh's loan and the monthly A$40,000 repayments to Ubertas.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid the unsupported claim that APG had faced financial pressure 'every month'.

fact_changes.current_value: On 18 May 2010, APG's landlord informed APG that its rent was more than three months in arrears.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In May 2010 it had difficulty paying its rent, and on 18 May 2010 its landlord alleged that it was more than three months in arrears of rent.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 77 says that in May 2010 APG had difficulty paying its rent, and on 18 May 2010 its landlord alleged it was more than three months in arrears.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Restore the source's broader wording and avoid presenting the landlord's allegation as an established fact.

fact_changes.current_value: Around 18 May 2010, Sean Colville Niven emailed APG's landlord asking for understanding and stating that APG, as a new business, had experienced cash flow struggles.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Mr Niven subsequently e-mailed the landlord asking for its understanding as 'as with many new businesses we have had some struggles with cash flow!'
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 77 quotes Mr Niven's email to the landlord in substance.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's quoted wording and avoid adding 'around 18 May 2010' if the source does not specify that exact date for the email.

fact_changes.current_value: On 24 June 2010, APG received a demand from its advertising agency to pay an outstanding amount of $59,502.92 by 4 July 2010, with a subsequent notice on 16 July 2010 that legal action would be taken if payment was not received by 19 July 2010.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 24 June 2010, APG received a demand from its advertising agency to pay an outstanding amount of $59,502.92 by 4 July 2010. On 16 July 2010, the agency informed APG that if the payment was not received by Monday, 19 July 2010, legal action would be taken against it.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 77 states both the 24 June demand and the 16 July warning.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Split the two events and keep the source's exact dates and wording.

fact_changes.current_value: In July 2010, APG's advertising agency agreed to accept payment of the outstanding $59,502.92 in two instalments, with $20,000 due by 20 July 2010 and the balance by 30 July 2010.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Subsequently, the agency agreed to payment in two instalments – $20,000 by 20 July 2010 and the balance by 30 July 2010.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 77 states the instalment arrangement in those terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid attributing the agreement to a specific month if not stated.

fact_changes.current_value: On 6 September 2010, APG, Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene executed a written agreement referred to as the September Agreement.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Following discussions on the company's financial situation, Mr Niven, Mr Wang, Ms Lim and APG executed an agreement dated 6 September 2010 ('the September Agreement').
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 12 states that the parties executed an agreement dated 6 September 2010 after discussions on the company's financial situation.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid implying the agreement was simply 'written' without the context given.

fact_changes.current_value: Under the 6 September 2010 agreement, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene agreed to capitalise $175,000 owed by APG to her and to inject an additional $150,000 into APG in exchange for 325,000 new APG shares.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Under the September Agreement, Ms Lim agreed to capitalise the amount of $175,000 owed by APG to her and inject a sum of $150,000 into the company. In exchange, she would receive 325,000 new shares in APG.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 12 states these terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid compressing the exchange into a single clause.

fact_changes.current_value: Under the 6 September 2010 agreement, Sean Colville Niven acknowledged owing APG $300,450.37 and it was recorded that APG would write off 40% of this amount, with the remaining $180,270.22 to be paid by him in two instalments by 31 December 2011 and 31 December 2012.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Under the September Agreement, Mr Niven acknowledged that he owed the company $300,450.37 and it was agreed that the company would write off 40% of this amount with Mr Niven paying the balance $180,270.22 in two instalments: 50% by 31 December 2011 and the remaining 50% by 31 December 2012.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 12 states the debt, write-off and instalment terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's exact instalment structure of 50%/50% rather than implying two fixed dates without the percentage split.

fact_changes.current_value: The 6 September 2010 agreement recorded that unpaid share capital liabilities were allocated as $560,000 for Sean Colville Niven, $160,000 for Wang Zijian and $80,000 for Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, and that shareholders would repay 10% of the outstanding capital each year for ten years.
fact_changes.recommended_value: The September Agreement also provided for the payment of the unpaid share capital. In this respect, the parties agreed that Mr Niven was liable for $560,000, Mr Wang was liable for $160,000 and Ms Lim for $80,000. By cl 1.3, it was provided that the shareholders had to repay the company 10% of the outstanding capital each year for ten years.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 12 states these terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid recasting the agreement as a general allocation of liabilities.

fact_changes.current_value: On 6 October 2010, by written directors' resolution signed by Sean Colville Niven and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, APG approved the appointment of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd to conduct a review of APG's accounts.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 6 October 2010, by a written directors' resolution signed by Mr Niven and Ms Lim, the company resolved that the appointment of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd to conduct a review of the company's accounts be approved.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 13 states the resolution in those terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'approved the appointment' as a paraphrase if not necessary.

fact_changes.current_value: On 11 October 2010, Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd sent APG's board a draft summary of findings from its preliminary review of APG's financial books and records, noting that many payments, including payments to Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, lacked supporting vouchers and that APG had paid for personal expenses of Sean Colville Niven.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 11 October 2010, FH sent the board its draft summary of findings from its preliminary review of its financial books and records. This review indicated that many of the company's payments, including to Mr Niven and Mr Wang, were not supported by vouchers, there was no clear indication of employees' and directors' benefits and entitlements and various professional fees had been paid without indicating any relevance to APG. Several payments had been made to Mr Niven in Australia by internet withdrawals and there had also been payments to an Australian company called 'Australian Property Group (Vic) Pty Ltd'. There were no documents to support these Australian transactions. Further, APG had paid for the personal expenses of Mr Niven.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 13 contains these findings.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Restore the fuller source description and avoid narrowing the findings to only some payments.

fact_changes.current_value: On 13 October 2010, after discussions among the three directors, a handwritten document referred to as the October Agreement was signed by Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene.
fact_changes.recommended_value: After receipt of the draft report, the three directors began discussions on parting ways. These resulted in a handwritten document which was signed by Mr Niven, Mr Wang and Ms Lim on 13 October 2010. This document will be referred to as the 'October Agreement'.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 14 states this sequence.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid implying the document was simply a standalone agreement without the preceding context.

fact_changes.current_value: Under the 13 October 2010 document, it was recorded that Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would resign as directors that day and transfer all their APG shares, except for 10% retained by Wang Zijian, to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, and that Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene would pay Sean Colville Niven's outstanding expenses and commission in instalments.
fact_changes.recommended_value: The October Agreement provided that Mr Niven and Mr Wang would resign as directors that very day and transfer all their shares, except for 10% in Mr Wang's hands, to Ms Lim. Ms Lim agreed to pay Mr Niven's outstanding expenses and commission in instalments.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 15 states these terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'APG shares' where the source simply says 'all their shares'.

fact_changes.current_value: The 13 October 2010 document recorded that upon settlement, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would have no further compliance-related liabilities to APG and would owe nothing to APG except for personal tax liabilities.
fact_changes.recommended_value: It was further agreed that Mr Niven and Mr Wang would have no liability to APG 'from a compliance perspective upon settlement' and that they 'will owe nothing to the company upon settlement, excluding any personal tax liabilities'.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 15 quotes the October Agreement.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's quoted wording rather than paraphrasing it as 'compliance-related liabilities'.

fact_changes.current_value: On or about 17 November 2009, three OCBC cheques were drawn by Sean Colville Niven: cheque number 771893 for $320,000 payable to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, cheque number 771397 for $300,000 payable to APG, and cheque number 771399 dated 1 December 2009 for $490,000 payable to APG.
fact_changes.recommended_value: The defendants' submissions appear to be referring to three cheques drawn by Mr Niven, viz, OCBC cheque No 771893 dated 17 November 2009 for the sum of $320,000 to Ms Lim, OCBC cheque No 771397 also dated 17 November 2009 for the sum of $300,000 to APG and OCBC cheque No 771399 dated 1 December 2009 for the sum of $490,000 to APG. There are no cheques dated 17 December 2009.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 42 states the cheque numbers and dates, and expressly says there are no cheques dated 17 December 2009.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Correct the date from 'on or about 17 November 2009' and preserve the source's note that there were no cheques dated 17 December 2009.

fact_changes.current_value: Around 1 December 2009, part of the proceeds of an A$450,000 loan from Ubertas was deposited into Sean Colville Niven's personal account in Australia and then transmitted in Singapore dollars to APG through cheques including the $490,000 and $300,000 cheques.
fact_changes.recommended_value: At least A$450,000 of the $490,000 cheque was actually the company's own money, being the loan proceeds from the Ubertas loan. This was subsequently confirmed by Mr Niven who agreed that part of the Ubertas loan had been deposited into his own account in Australia and he had then made payment in Singapore dollars to APG.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraphs 44 and 45 state that at least A$450,000 of the $490,000 cheque was the company's own money and that Mr Niven agreed part of the Ubertas loan had been deposited into his own account and then paid to APG.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported 'around 1 December 2009' framing and align the description with the source's more precise explanation.

fact_changes.current_value: On 22 February 2010, APG issued an invoice to Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd for A$86,000 described as marketing funds for a property marketing campaign, with instructions on the invoice for payment to be made to Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In February 2010, APG carried out a marketing campaign for a property being developed by Ubertas 350. On 22 February 2010, APG raised an invoice to Ubertas 350 for the sum of A$86,000 to cover the marketing funds for the campaign. Mr Niven signed the invoice and inserted instructions on it for the funds to be paid to his personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 36 states these facts.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid implying the invoice itself was 'issued to' the company in a way that obscures the campaign context.

fact_changes.current_value: Following the 22 February 2010 invoice, Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd paid A$86,000 into Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In due course, Ubertas 350 paid A$86,000 to Mr Niven's account.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 36 states that Ubertas 350 paid A$86,000 to Mr Niven's account.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'personal bank account in Australia' if not necessary.

fact_changes.current_value: On 6 February 2009, a joint current account with United Overseas Bank Ltd, account number 339xxx, was opened in the joint names of Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In February 2009, the defendants had set up a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx ('the defendants' joint account').
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 90 states that in February 2009 the defendants had set up a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported exact opening date of 6 February 2009.

fact_changes.current_value: Between 6 February 2009 and 31 May 2009, funds were regularly transferred from APG's bank account into the joint UOB account held by Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Copies of the monthly bank statements for the defendants' joint account for the period from 6 February 2009 to 31 May 2009 were adduced in evidence. These statements revealed that funds were regularly transferred from APG's bank account to the defendants' joint account.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 90 states the statement period and the transfers.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid implying the transfers occurred only within that exact date range as a factual finding beyond the statement period.

fact_changes.current_value: On 8 December 2009, APG's board, then comprising Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, passed a resolution allowing cheques drawn on APG's bank account to be signed by a single signatory, either Sean Colville Niven or Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene.
fact_changes.recommended_value: By 8 December 2009, the NEDs were out of the picture and the new board consisted of the defendants and Ms Lim. On that date, they passed a directors' resolution to allow cheques drawn on APG's bank account to be signed by a single signatory, being either Mr Niven or Ms Lim.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 91 states the board composition and the resolution.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid over-specifying the board as 'then comprising' if not necessary.

fact_changes.current_value: In November 2010, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene repaid the outstanding Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of APG's debt to Ubertas, thereby becoming APG's creditor in place of Ubertas.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In November 2010, Ms Lim repaid the Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of the outstanding amount.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 72 states that in November 2010 Ms Lim repaid the Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of the outstanding amount.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported inference that she thereby became APG's creditor in place of Ubertas, which is not stated in the source.

fact_changes.current_value: From May 2010, Wang Zijian was based in Malaysia and was no longer involved in APG's daily operations and management matters.
fact_changes.recommended_value: From May 2010 Mr Wang had been in Malaysia and had no longer been involved in the daily operations or management matters of APG.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 87 states this fact.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid stating it as a definitive relocation if the source only says he had been in Malaysia.

fact_changes.current_value: On 1 March 2011, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene applied to place APG under judicial management.
fact_changes.evidence: This is litigation-stage procedural conduct occurring after the dispute had clearly arisen and after the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove this procedural litigation fact from the pre-dispute factual row.

fact_changes.current_value: On 21 March 2011, APG was placed under judicial management and Timothy James Reid of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd was appointed as judicial manager.
fact_changes.evidence: This is a court/procedural outcome and appointment occurring after the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove this litigation-stage procedural fact.

fact_changes.current_value: In July 2011, APG, acting through its judicial manager, commenced this action to recover money paid to the defendants.
fact_changes.evidence: This is commencement of legal proceedings and therefore beyond the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove this litigation filing fact.

fact_changes.current_value: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and financial arrangements, payments made from APG's funds to or for the benefit of Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, and the non-payment of share capital for their APG shares, correspond with APG's claims for recovery of those sums and unpaid capital.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Whether APG's pre-dispute conduct and financial arrangements show that payments made from APG's funds to or for the benefit of Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, and the non-payment of share capital for their APG shares, correspond with APG's claims for recovery of those sums and unpaid capital.
fact_changes.evidence: The issue field should be neutral background only; it should not reference the litigation claims framing in a way that imports post-dispute posture.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Rephrase to neutral background and avoid claim-framing language tied to the lawsuit.

fact_changes.current_value: Company directors and shareholders are generally pay for shares they subscribe and to ensure that company funds are used for company purposes in accordance with agreed remuneration and financing arrangements. When a company is in financial difficulty, decisions that reduce assets available to meet existing obligations are take into account the position of those to whom the company owes money.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Company directors and shareholders are generally required to pay for shares they subscribe and to ensure that company funds are used for company purposes in accordance with agreed remuneration and financing arrangements.
fact_changes.evidence: The rule field should preserve only neutral background legal principles needed to understand the row and avoid outcome-oriented or judgment-stage framing.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the insolvency/creditor-interest sentence because it reflects judgment-stage legal reasoning rather than neutral background.

fact_changes.current_value: APG's records shares were issued to Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian as fully paid although the consideration was not in fact paid, that APG received loan funding from Goh Cheng Liang and later from Ubertas Funds Management Pty Ltd, and that APG experienced cash flow problems in 2010 including rent arrears and creditor demands. During this period, funds were transferred from APG's bank account to a joint account of Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, APG funds were used to pay expenses associated with Sean Colville Niven, and an invoice for A$86,000 in marketing funds was issued with instructions for payment into his personal account. Agreements in September and October 2010 recorded write-offs and rescheduling of amounts owed to APG and reallocation of unpaid share capital obligations while APG was facing cash flow pressure. These pre-dispute events form the factual basis for APG's claims to recover payments and unpaid capital from the defendants.
fact_changes.recommended_value: APG's records show that shares were issued to Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian as fully paid although the consideration was not in fact paid, that APG received loan funding from Goh Cheng Liang and later from Ubertas Funds Management Pty Ltd, and that APG experienced cash flow problems in 2010 including rent arrears and creditor demands. During this period, funds were transferred from APG's bank account to a joint account of Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, APG funds were used to pay expenses associated with Sean Colville Niven, and an invoice for A$86,000 in marketing funds was issued with instructions for payment into his personal account. Agreements in September and October 2010 recorded write-offs and rescheduling of amounts owed to APG and reallocation of unpaid share capital obligations while APG was facing cash flow pressure.
fact_changes.evidence: The application field should be limited to pre-dispute background and should not frame the facts as the basis of APG's claims.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the final sentence that explicitly ties the facts to the lawsuit's claims.

fact_changes.current_value:
The judge analysed APG's business model, the structure of Front End and Back End Commissions, and the defendants' roles. The judgment found that APG's funds had been used for Sean Colville Niven's personal benefit, including American Express card bills, personal legal fees, undocumented payments, family travel expenses, payments to third parties for personal items, and the A$86,000 received from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd. The court treated appropriation of company funds for personal use as a prima facie abuse of funds and applied authorities such as Creanovate Pte Ltd v Firstlink Energy Pte Ltd. The judge rejected the defendants' contentions that an Account Practice and an Oral Agreement authorised these payments, finding no contemporaneous vouchers or corroborating evidence and noting inconsistencies with clause 5 of the Goh loan agreement and the conduct of the non-executive directors. The court concluded that the alleged oral arrangements were not established.

On insolvency, the judge relied on the 2 July 2010 email from John Kennedy Stuart describing APG as technically insolvent, Sean Colville Niven's email responses acknowledging the creditors and revenue reports, evidence of rent arrears, creditor demands from the advertising agency, and the need for cash injections. The court held that APG was insolvent or near insolvency by September and October 2010. Applying principles from Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and Liquidators of Progen Engineering Pte Ltd v Progen Holdings Ltd, the judge held that when a company is insolvent or in a parlous financial position, directors must consider creditors' interests and avoid transactions that dissipate assets to the prejudice of creditors.

The judge examined the September and October Agreements and found that they required APG to write off a substantial portion of Sean Colville Niven's debt, assume half of his personal debt to the non-executive directors, rearrange and postpone payment of unpaid share capital over ten years, and effectively release the defendants from liabilities to APG without corresponding benefit to APG or its creditors. The court held that these agreements were not in the interests of APG's creditors and that the directors, including Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, acted in breach of their duties in entering into them. The agreements were treated as voidable and set aside, so they did not provide a defence to APG's claims or a basis for the counterclaims.

Regarding unpaid share capital, the judge found that neither Sean Colville Niven nor Wang Zijian had paid for their original share allotments. The court rejected the argument that certain payments in November and December 2009 constituted share capital payments, noting that they largely represented transmission of Ubertas loan funds, were not pleaded as capital payments, and were inconsistent with the September Agreement in which Sean Colville Niven accepted liability for unpaid capital of $560,000. The court held that Sean Colville Niven remained liable for unpaid capital on the shares he continued to hold (400,000 shares, including 40,000 acquired from the non-executive directors) but not for the 240,000 shares transferred to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene before the call, as liability for calls follows the registered holder at the time of the call under APG's articles. Wang Zijian was held liable for $160,000 in unpaid capital on his 160,000 shares.

On liability for the payments, the judge held that the defendants had not displaced the prima facie conclusion that the payments for Sean Colville Niven's personal benefit were improper. The court rejected the argument that whatever benefited the 'rain-maker' was necessarily in APG's interest and applied an objective test of whether an honest and diligent person could reasonably regard the transactions as beneficial to the company. The court found that many expenses were clearly personal, lacked supporting documentation, or were not shown to be incurred for APG's business. The judge held that Sean Colville Niven was liable to repay the sums claimed, subject to deduction of amounts already recovered from the first defendant law firm.

As to Wang Zijian, the court distinguished between his lack of personal benefit from the impugned payments and his responsibilities as a director. The judge found that he did not control APG's cheque books after the single-signatory resolution and that he was not directly responsible for cheques drawn solely by Sean Colville Niven. However, because funds were transferred from APG's account into the joint UOB account held by both defendants, the court held that both defendants had to account jointly and severally for sums paid into that joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes. The court declined to treat general allegations about accounting systems as a separate pleaded claim for damages.

The judge did not make findings on alleged misconduct by Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, noting that her liability was not in issue in this action and that any wrongdoing by her would not absolve the defendants. The court dismissed the defendants' counterclaims, which were premised on the validity of the September Agreement. In the result, the court ordered that Sean Colville Niven pay APG $400,000 for unpaid share capital and $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10 for payments for his personal use (less amounts recovered from the first defendant), that Wang Zijian pay APG $160,000 for unpaid share capital, and that both defendants jointly and severally account for and, where not justified, repay sums paid into their joint account, with interest at the court rate and costs to APG.

facts_to_remove.current_value:
The judge analysed APG's business model, the structure of Front End and Back End Commissions, and the defendants' roles. The judgment found that APG's funds had been used for Sean Colville Niven's personal benefit, including American Express card bills, personal legal fees, undocumented payments, family travel expenses, payments to third parties for personal items, and the A$86,000 received from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd. The court treated appropriation of company funds for personal use as a prima facie abuse of funds and applied authorities such as Creanovate Pte Ltd v Firstlink Energy Pte Ltd. The judge rejected the defendants' contentions that an Account Practice and an Oral Agreement authorised these payments, finding no contemporaneous vouchers or corroborating evidence and noting inconsistencies with clause 5 of the Goh loan agreement and the conduct of the non-executive directors. The court concluded that the alleged oral arrangements were not established.

On insolvency, the judge relied on the 2 July 2010 email from John Kennedy Stuart describing APG as technically insolvent, Sean Colville Niven's email responses acknowledging the creditors and revenue reports, evidence of rent arrears, creditor demands from the advertising agency, and the need for cash injections. The court held that APG was insolvent or near insolvency by September and October 2010. Applying principles from Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and Liquidators of Progen Engineering Pte Ltd v Progen Holdings Ltd, the judge held that when a company is insolvent or in a parlous financial position, directors must consider creditors' interests and avoid transactions that dissipate assets to the prejudice of creditors.

The judge examined the September and October Agreements and found that they required APG to write off a substantial portion of Sean Colville Niven's debt, assume half of his personal debt to the non-executive directors, rearrange and postpone payment of unpaid share capital over ten years, and effectively release the defendants from liabilities to APG without corresponding benefit to APG or its creditors. The court held that these agreements were not in the interests of APG's creditors and that the directors, including Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, acted in breach of their duties in entering into them. The agreements were treated as voidable and set aside, so they did not provide a defence to APG's claims or a basis for the counterclaims.

Regarding unpaid share capital, the judge found that neither Sean Colville Niven nor Wang Zijian had paid for their original share allotments. The court rejected the argument that certain payments in November and December 2009 constituted share capital payments, noting that they largely represented transmission of Ubertas loan funds, were not pleaded as capital payments, and were inconsistent with the September Agreement in which Sean Colville Niven accepted liability for unpaid capital of $560,000. The court held that Sean Colville Niven remained liable for unpaid capital on the shares he continued to hold (400,000 shares, including 40,000 acquired from the non-executive directors) but not for the 240,000 shares transferred to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene before the call, as liability for calls follows the registered holder at the time of the call under APG's articles. Wang Zijian was held liable for $160,000 in unpaid capital on his 160,000 shares.

On liability for the payments, the judge held that the defendants had not displaced the prima facie conclusion that the payments for Sean Colville Niven's personal benefit were improper. The court rejected the argument that whatever benefited the 'rain-maker' was necessarily in APG's interest and applied an objective test of whether an honest and diligent person could reasonably regard the transactions as beneficial to the company. The court found that many expenses were clearly personal, lacked supporting documentation, or were not shown to be incurred for APG's business. The judge held that Sean Colville Niven was liable to repay the sums claimed, subject to deduction of amounts already recovered from the first defendant law firm.

As to Wang Zijian, the court distinguished between his lack of personal benefit from the impugned payments and his responsibilities as a director. The judge found that he did not control APG's cheque books after the single-signatory resolution and that he was not directly responsible for cheques drawn solely by Sean Colville Niven. However, because funds were transferred from APG's account into the joint UOB account held by both defendants, the court held that both defendants had to account jointly and severally for sums paid into that joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes. The court declined to treat general allegations about accounting systems as a separate pleaded claim for damages.

The judge did not make findings on alleged misconduct by Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, noting that her liability was not in issue in this action and that any wrongdoing by her would not absolve the defendants. The court dismissed the defendants' counterclaims, which were premised on the validity of the September Agreement. In the result, the court ordered that Sean Colville Niven pay APG $400,000 for unpaid share capital and $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10 for payments for his personal use (less amounts recovered from the first defendant), that Wang Zijian pay APG $160,000 for unpaid share capital, and that both defendants jointly and severally account for and, where not justified, repay sums paid into their joint account, with interest at the court rate and costs to APG.

facts_to_remove.current_value: APG's incorporation records Wang Zijian was allotted 160,000 shares described as fully paid although he later acknowledged that he had never paid for those shares. The September Agreement records his unpaid share capital liability as $160,000 with a ten-year repayment schedule. Bank statements APG funds were transferred into a joint UOB account held by him and Sean Colville Niven, and he had access to that account. From May 2010 he was no longer involved in APG's daily operations, and there is no indication that he personally incurred the specific expenses challenged by APG. These pre-dispute facts support APG's position that he remains responsible for the unpaid capital on his 160,000 shares and that he must, together with Sean Colville Niven, account for APG funds paid into their joint account.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This contains litigation-stage argument and outcome-oriented analysis.

facts_to_remove.current_value:
The judge accepted that there was no allegation that Wang Zijian personally benefited from the specific impugned payments for Sean Colville Niven's expenses. However, the court emphasised that as a director he owed duties to APG and could be responsible where he facilitated or was involved in improper payments. The evidence showed that a joint UOB account was opened in the names of both defendants and that APG funds were regularly transferred into this account, allowing them to bypass the requirement for non-executive directors' signatures on APG cheques. The court held that both defendants were jointly and severally liable to account for sums paid into this joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes.

Regarding unpaid share capital, the court noted that Wang Zijian admitted he had never paid for his 160,000 shares. The September Agreement recorded his unpaid capital liability as $160,000. The judge held that, subject to the invalidity of the September and October Agreements as defences, he remained liable for the unpaid capital on his shares. Applying the principle that calls on unpaid share capital attach to the registered holder at the time of the call, and noting that he remained the registered holder of 160,000 shares when the call was made in March 2011, the court ordered him to pay $160,000 to APG. The court rejected his argument that responsibility should rest solely with Sean Colville Niven and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, distinguishing between his lack of personal benefit from the challenged expenses and his obligations as a director and shareholder in relation to share capital and funds transferred into the joint account.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is entirely judgment reasoning and outcome-stage material.
